title: Programming beacons
html:
Beacons are the small clues in code that trigger an assumption about what is going on — an assumption that, most of the time, turns out to be right. Because they let your brain skip ahead with reasonable confidence, they save you some mental expense: instead of reading every line to reconstruct meaning, you recognize a familiar signal and move on.

A beacon is often just a common naming convention. For example, if a variable is named “node”, the odds are good that it is an element of a tree, a graph, or some other connected collection type. You didn’t have to trace its origin to form that expectation; the name itself did the work. Good code is full of these quiet signposts, and reading it feels faster precisely because the beacons keep confirming what you already guessed.

You can deliberately enrich your code by glittering it with beacons. When the right cue is hard to implant in the statements themselves — when a name or structure can’t quite carry the hint — drop it into a comment instead, so the next reader inherits the same shortcut you would want. (A small idea I keep returning to from a friend’s thinking.)

Though, Does code still run on people?.

backlinks.title: Your rich and consistent vocabulary is the best API for your second brain
backlinks.context: • § Articulation skill - Programming beacons - Does code still run on people?
